behaviors.description: The conversation history passed to the LLM is trimmed to the most recent 10 messages when the full history exceeds the context window.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is the handoff between history-trimming logic and LLM request construction (`passed to the LLM`), not only a pure helper function. A unit test of a trim function could miss ordering/serialization bugs at module boundaries. No real OS/network/subprocess is required, and correctness does not depend on model output quality, so System/Agentic are rejected.
behaviors.test_description: Wire conversation service + prompt builder + LLM client adapter with an in-process fake LLM endpoint that records payloads. Feed >10-message history and assert the outbound LLM request contains exactly the latest 10 messages in correct order/roles.

behaviors.description: The generated response addresses the customer's question rather than producing a generic or off-topic reply.
behaviors.justification: This is output-quality validation of the model response content (`addresses the customer's question` vs off-topic). Deterministic stubs would hide real failures because they replace model judgment. Multiple-step pipeline is not required by this behavior text, so Workflow is rejected.
behaviors.test_description: Run real prompts against the production-like LLM with a labeled eval set of customer questions; score relevance/answerability (human rubric or model-graded with adjudication) and fail if relevance falls below threshold or off-topic rate exceeds budget.

behaviors.description: The tone re-check correctly identifies when a response is too formal for a casual-tier customer and triggers regeneration.
behaviors.justification: `correctly identifies` tone suitability is a model/classifier judgment problem; mocked outputs can always pass and hide real misclassification. Regeneration trigger is orchestration, but the key failure here is correctness of the tone decision itself. Workflow was considered but rejected because this behavior does not require validating end-to-end quality across multiple agentic decisions, only the tone-check decision plus trigger.
behaviors.test_description: Use real LLM tone-check path on a curated set of intentionally over-formal replies for casual-tier users; verify high recall of `too formal` flags and assert regeneration path is invoked when flagged.

behaviors.description: The full exchange (user message, generated response, any regenerations) is persisted to the database with correct timestamps.
behaviors.justification: This is multi-module state persistence (chat flow + persistence layer + DB schema/timestamp handling). A pure unit test can miss transaction and mapping errors. Real external infrastructure is not required because a local test DB can expose these failures; model output quality is irrelevant, so Agentic/Workflow are rejected.
behaviors.test_description: Execute chat flow with deterministic LLM stubs (including forced regeneration) against a real local test DB, then assert stored rows include user message, first draft, regenerated draft(s), linkage/order, and correct timestamp fields.

behaviors.description: When the tone check triggers regeneration, the second response replaces the first — the customer never sees the rejected draft.
behaviors.justification: This is cross-stage agent pipeline behavior with sequential LLM-involved steps (initial draft -> tone gate -> regeneration -> user-visible output). Real failures often appear in handoff/state selection across steps, not in isolated modules. Integration with single-step stubs can miss sequencing bugs where the rejected draft leaks. System is not required (no OS/network-specific failure needed).
behaviors.test_description: Run end-to-end chat workflow with real model calls for initial generation and regeneration on scenarios that trigger tone re-check; assert externally returned/customer-visible message is always the regenerated one and the rejected draft is only internal/logged.
